text:
I just watched the FOSDEM'19 talk about SOLID and found it very interesting. I'd be happy to hear your thoughts on this topic.
<https://fosdem.org/2019/schedule/event/solid_web_decentralization/>
Direct link to the video: <https://ftp.heanet.ie/mirrors/fosdem-video/2019/Janson/solid_web_decentralization.webm>

text: Love this unusual take on visual programming: <http://www.hempuli.com/baba/>

When you design a programming language it's important to ensure that it is convenient.
Seems like the best way to do it is to write a program in this imaginary language.

What are good problems to try out with this imaginary language?

 * a backend for blog web app,
 * a backend for webchat with websockets

any other suggestions?

any other suggestions?

• not a compiler
• not a query to find ancestors in a family
• not Fibonacci
• not shape inheritance

• not a compiler
• not a query to find ancestors in a family
• not Fibonacci
• not shape inheritance
:stuck_out_tongue:
:stuck_out_tongue:

depends a lot on the target audience of the language. From examples you've listed, it looks like your audience is backend developers, but not necessarily focused on distributed programming. But this might not be the audience you initially target, so you'd probably need to pick examples that the audience would relate to the most

depends a lot on the target audience of the language. From examples you've listed, it looks like your audience is backend developers, but not necessarily focused on distributed programming. But this might not be the audience you initially target, so you'd probably need to pick examples that the audience would relate to the most

Depends also on what the motivation for the language is, and it sounds like you are implicitly describing the case of server languages?

If I am to create a language it is either because it should be a DSL that neatly describes a specific existing problem space, therefore examples would follow from that, OR it is because I am interested in trying to do “new categories of things” that I am unlikely to be able to describe accurately before the language has been prototyped. I think this is why Bret Victor got excited about Laser Socks.

I want it to be ran on server, yes. But I don't limit it to it.

I have a list of principles that I want to be followed during design of this language. I have list of features that I believe would support those principles.

Now I want to imagine that this language already exist and write a program.

So you have principles and features, but what is the overall motivation?
So you have principles and features, but what is the overall motivation?
Make programming more efficient in terms of human-computer interaction.
Make programming more efficient in terms of human-computer interaction.
Use abstractions that easy to work with. Abstractions that play along with visual interface. Abstractions that play along with collaboration.
Use abstractions that easy to work with. Abstractions that play along with visual interface. Abstractions that play along with collaboration.
Can you share your list of principles and features <@UCKRZS3DZ> ?
Can you share your list of principles and features <@UCKRZS3DZ> ?

Efficient in what way? What does “easy to work with” mean, and who does it apply to? I’m not demanding answers, just suggesting questions that might help you towards more example programs :slightly_smiling_face:

Efficient in what way? What does “easy to work with” mean, and who does it apply to? I’m not demanding answers, just suggesting questions that might help you towards more example programs :slightly_smiling_face:

<@UEGFXUK3M> efficient in sense that people spend less time solving it.

Faster to grasp what unknown program does.
Faster to locate what part of the system causes the problem.
Faster to iterate in development cycle: run, change.

I see problems in abstractions that we use. They allow too much flexibility, have too many dependencies.

<@UEGFXUK3M> efficient in sense that people spend less time solving it.

Faster to grasp what unknown program does.
Faster to locate what part of the system causes the problem.
Faster to iterate in development cycle: run, change.

I see problems in abstractions that we use. They allow too much flexibility, have too many dependencies.
<@U85HCL7JP> I need to write down a summary
<@U85HCL7JP> I need to write down a summary

<@U85HCL7JP> something like that:

 * Collaboration is the key to efficiency. Everything should be easy to share, to detach, to attach, to reproduce. Anything is possible to comment, tag, bookmark, reference.

 * structure based source code stored in something like database. Code edited through browser, there are no text files on disk
 * basic data abstraction is DAG. Fits well with file systems, with source code presentation. Lists, tuples and dicts are perfectly expressed by DAG.
 * peers are working in same workspace connected to each other. All code changes are always stored. There is no such thing as uncommited code. Everything is searchable.
 * there are functions and actors. Communication between actors happens through channels.
 * program is just a graph of actors that connected through channels.
 * visualization of the data is just another actor that stored in shared workspace.
 * Complicated system/service consists of several layers of programs. Layer above can manipulate parts of level below, but cannon break inside parts. This reminds a lot composition of DSLs, but not really. Haven't figured out exactly how it should work yet.

 * basic data abstraction is DAG. Fits well with file systems, with source code presentation. Lists, tuples and dicts are perfectly expressed by DAG.
 * peers are working in same workspace connected to each other. All code changes are always stored. There is no such thing as uncommited code. Everything is searchable.
 * there are functions and actors. Communication between actors happens through channels.
 * program is just a graph of actors that connected through channels.
 * visualization of the data is just another actor that stored in shared workspace.
 * Complicated system/service consists of several layers of programs. Layer above can manipulate parts of level below, but cannon break inside parts. This reminds a lot composition of DSLs, but not really. Haven't figured out exactly how it should work yet.
Sounds cool!
Sounds cool!

> Faster to grasp what unknown program does.
> Faster to locate what part of the system causes the problem.
> Faster to iterate in development cycle: run, change.

It seems from this that the example program is less relevant than the scenario that would allow you demonstrate these activities. E.g. “someone else’s program”, “a program with a problem”, “several versions of the same program”. So it could be just one example program, demonstrating these activities. If this was a demo, then your audience would start to forget about the specific example and think more about the properties of the language? Just a thought!

For now I just want to check that I picked up set of abstractions correctly. Ensure that functions + actors + DAG data is enough and convenient.

text: Not a todo list app?
Not a todo list app?
Although I think that's actually a good minimally useful app to show off a new GUI framework
Although I think that's actually a good minimally useful app to show off a new GUI framework

As a game developer I like to see small non-trivial games, like a simple shmup (space invaders) or platformer (mario) or if its 3D maybe an FPS (doom).  None of those really apply to something focused on backend however.

As a game developer I like to see small non-trivial games, like a simple shmup (space invaders) or platformer (mario) or if its 3D maybe an FPS (doom).  None of those really apply to something focused on backend however.
A photo sharing app might be a good example, especially if it does some simple batch processing on photos (resize of blur or something)

Thanks, these are good ideas.
Thanks, these are good ideas.

As I understand it, Solid is based around protocols that basically require people to run their own servers to participate in the network.

Personally, I find <https://beakerbrowser.com/> to have a much more compelling vision.
Victoria has shown me some demos of the stuff they're doing at Adobe. Really cool stuff! 3D capture is a great way to lower the technical bar for making animations.
